coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient photoanode in visible light water splitting through development of Z-scheme structure between compositing TiO2 with GQDs and Ba doped VO2 (m) with smart selection of Ag nanoparticles sites
coredata.dc:creator: Sajjadizadeh, Halimeh-Sadat
Goharshadi, Elaheh K.
Karimi-Nazarabad, Mahdi
coredata.dc:description: A highly efficient photoanode for water splitting under visible light is designed by placing Ag nanoparticles (NPs) at the interface of Ba doped VO2 (m) (BaV) and compositing hierarchical porous TiO2 NPs with a graphene quantum dots layer (TG). In fact, the electron flow direction in the photoanode is simply adjusted to develop the Z-scheme structure through solution processing assembly. A very high photocurrent of 2.15 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE is achieved for sequentially assembled reduced graphene oxide (RGO)/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode. This value is 30 times higher than that of pristine TiO2 photoanode (0.07 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE) and a 70 % increase over type-II heterojunction with RGO/Ag@TG/BaV assembly. Importantly, the stability of this Z-scheme photoanode is 10 times higher than its type-II heterojunction counterpart, making it a promising option for practical applications in water splitting under visible light.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient photoanode in visible light water splitting through development of Z-scheme structure between compositing TiO2 with GQDs and Ba doped VO2 (m) with smart selection of Ag nanoparticles sites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly efficient photoanode for water splitting under visible light is designed by placing Ag nanoparticles (NPs) at the interface of Ba doped VO2 (m) (BaV) and compositing hierarchical porous TiO2 NPs with a graphene quantum dots layer (TG). In fact, the electron flow direction in the photoanode is simply adjusted to develop the Z-scheme structure through solution processing assembly. A very high photocurrent of 2.15 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE is achieved for sequentially assembled reduced graphene oxide (RGO)/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode. This value is 30 times higher than that of pristine TiO2 photoanode (0.07 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE) and a 70 % increase over type-II heterojunction with RGO/Ag@TG/BaV assembly. Importantly, the stability of this Z-scheme photoanode is 10 times higher than its type-II heterojunction counterpart, making it a promising option for practical applications in water splitting under visible light.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: An attractive solution for accommodating global energy needs is the use of solar energy to produce hydrogen via photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting with semiconductor materials as photoelectrocatalysts [1–6]. To date, TiO2 has been emerged as stable, low-cost, and safe photocatalyst [7,8]. Much research efforts have been devoted to extend the photo-response of TiO2 to visible region and suppress the recombination of photo-induced carriers [9–12].

Different approach, including engineering of nanostructure [13,14], elemental doping [15–19], and development of composite or heterostructures [20–25], have been applied to better the visible light absorption efficiency, charge separation and transport of TiO2. Recently, our research group benefited from the synergetic effects of doping, compositing, and coupling strategies to build high-performance TiO2 photoanodes for water splitting [22,23]. We found that Ag doped hierarchical porous TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) and compositing with graphene quantum dots (Ag@TG) leads to design an excellent photoanode for PEC water splitting by modifying visible-light absorption and promoting carrier separation of TiO2. Also, a remarkable photocurrent density (Jph, 1.4 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE) with a 20-fold increase in comparison with pristine TiO2 photoanode (0.07 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs RHE) was obtained by formation of heterostructure (type-II heterojunction) between Ag@TG with Ba doped VO2 (m) (BaV) mainly due to their effective spatial separation of electron and hole (e−/h+) pairs via the band alignment between Ag@TG with Ba doped VO2 (m) (BaV) [22].

The high photocurrent density in Ag@TG/BaV photoanode conducts us to further improvement of the photocurrent by promoting separation and transfer of charge at the interface Ag@TG and BaV layer and increasing the redox ability of charge carriers. A high efficiency of charge carrier separation and transfer can be attained by using an electron mediator at the interface of two semiconductors resulting in assembling an indirect Z-scheme or all-solid-state Z-scheme system [26,27]. In comparison with to the type-II heterojunction system, the Z-scheme system is better because of facilitating the electron flow between two semiconductors through an electron transport chain known as the mediator. Noble metals and some carbonaceous materials are generally applied as electron mediators for designing all-solid-state Z-scheme systems because of the great charge mobility and wider spectral absorption range [28,29]. Moreover, a Z-scheme system simultaneously maintains a highly reductive and oxidative ability for photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen/oxygen evolution reaction due to its high conduction/low valence band position and strong photostability [30,31]. Tada et al. [32] found that the three-component nanojunction of CdS–Au–TiO2 has more active than that of either the single-component or the two-component of Au-TiO2 and TiO2-CdS systems. They claimed that by presence of Au NPs as an electron-transfer system between TiO2 and CdS, the separation of photogenerated charge carriers in TiO2 and CdS promotes so that the methylviologen reduction yield for CdS–Au–TiO2 nanojunction reached 52.2 %. The Z-scheme type of CdS–Au–TiO2 hollow nanorod arrays were prepared and used for the degradation of methylene blue in an aqueous solution.[33] Their results showed that CdS–Au–TiO2 three-component hollow nanorod arrays have significant photocatalytic activity (about 72 %) compared with their single component (15 %) and two components (25 or 35 %) systems. Fu’s group [34] found that TiO2 and Cu2O coupled photocatalysts have a relatively poor photocatalytic performance. For this purpose, they added Ag NPs into the interfacial contact of TiO2 and Cu2O. The photocatalytic efficiency of the Z-scheme TiO2–Ag–Cu2O system improved due to the preservation of the photo-induced holes on valence band (VB) of TiO2 with more positive potential and electrons on conduction band (CB) of Cu2O with more negative potential. In fact, the redox ability as well as the charge separation efficiency of the system as a whole enhances. Zouset al. [35] fabricated g-C3N4/Au/C-TiO2 hollow spheres as an all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalyst. This photocatalyst exhibited high H2 generation rate of 129.0 μmol g−1h−1 in visible light, which was 86 and 42 times greater than those of pure C-TiO2 and g-C3N4. The Jph for the Z-scheme system with graphene film as the electron mediator interlayer contacted with both TiO2 nanotube array and CdS quantum dots on two sides was about 204 % higher than that of TiO2 nanotube. This enhancement revealed that photogenerated electrons can more efficiently transfer from this Z-scheme to the counter electrode through the external circuit [36].

In our previous work, we identified the reduced graphene oxide (RGO)/Ag@TG/BaV photoanode as an excellent candidate for PEC water oxidation [22]. Building on these encouraging results, we investigated the Ag NPs sites and their alignment of conduction and valence bands of TiO2 and VO2. By using Ag NPs as an electron transfer mediator between the Ag@TG and BaV layers, we were able to develop a Z-scheme structure that further enhanced the performance of the photoanode. The sequentially assembled RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode promoted e−/h+ separation and facilitated the flow of electrons towards the counter electrode, resulting in long-term stability.

In addition, the photoanode enabled photo-induced holes on more positive potential (VB of Ag@TG) to participate in the water oxidation reaction, while electrons on more negative potential (CB of BaV) could be transferred to the counter electrode. This outstandingly enhanced the redox ability and the charge separation efficiency of the photoanode.

The ultrasonic cleaner dsa100-sk2 (Fuzhou Desen Precision Instruments Co., Ltd) and the ultrasonic processor (MISONIX model: S-4000) were employed for the fabrication of the nanomaterials (NMs) and photoelectrodes. Sample annealing was performed by the CVD instrument (CVD-33 NanoSAT). The spin coater (2M.T.D.I.92) was utilized for layer deposition. Drying and heating of samples were carried out using vacuum oven (Bench-top VS-1202V5) and oven (EF-2007 PAAT-ARIYA), respectively. The physical vapor deposition (PVD) of Ag NPs was performed by a thermal deposition evaporator (Edwards E306) that the tungsten crucible and its silver granule contents are placed in a vacuum chamber with pressure typically below 10−6 m bar. The solvent was removed from as-prepared samples using the freeze dryer (ALPHA 1–2 LD plus) in −50 °C and 0.05 mbar. The photoelectrochemical tests were acquired by potentiostat instrument (SAMA500 Research Center). The Mott–Schottky and impedance analysis were obtained by an Autolab (PGSTAT302N).

The phase purity and crystalline phase of the fabricated NMs were investigated by X-ray diffractometer (Bruker/D8 Advanced diffractometer) by Cu Kα radiation. The spectrophotometer (Photonix Ar 2015) was used for recording of UV–Vis absorption spectra of the samples. To record the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the photoelectrocatalys, we used from a Perkin Elemer LS45 fluorescence with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The transmission electron microscopy images (TEM) were provided on a JEM-2010 instrument. The morphology of prepared NMs and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis were determined using TESCAN MIRA3TESCAN-XMU field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM).

The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in a positive direction (−0.10 to 2.00 V vs. RHE was measured for plotting the photocurrent density-potential (Jph-E) plots. The photocurrent density (Jph) has been analyzed to assess the photoelectrode efficiency because it is proportional to the quantity of generated gas [37–39]. The chronoamperometry was measured under the potential of 1.23 V vs. RHE.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured under dark at 1.23 V vs. RHE. The range of frequency for ESI was 10−2–105 Hz. The Mott-Schottky analysis was performed in the range of the bias potential from −0.7 to 1.5 V vs. Ag/AgCl at the frequencies from 200 to 600 Hz.

The intrinsic solar to chemical conversion efficiency (ISTC) to assess the established maximum power by photoanodes was computed using the following equation [40,41]:

where Jphoto, Vphoto, and Vdark are the difference between light and dark currents, the potential difference between the light and dark current, and the required voltage to attain the dark current density is Jphoto . The Faradaic efficiency of approximately 100 % is supposed for the prepared photoanodes.

The flat band potential, Efb, was calculated from the intercept of Mott-Schottky plots according to Eq. (2) [42]:

where Csc , ε, ε0, A, e, k, and T are the capacitance of the space charge region, the dielectric constant of the semiconductor (which is 31 for anatase TiO2 [43] and 41 for VO2 [44]), permittivity in vacuum, the surface area, the electron charge, Boltzmann's constant, and absolute temperature, respectively. The conduction band potential, ECB, of a semiconductor is equal to the flat band, Efb , of an n-type semiconductor, while the valence band potential, EVB, of a semiconductor is equal to the E fb of a p-type semiconductor. The band gap potential, Eg, and the value of ECB can be obtained through Tauc and Mott-Schottky plots, respectively. The EVB can be determined using the formula of Eg = EVB – ECB. Then, the band diagram of photoelectrode can be drawn by these data.

Ag NPs were obtained via chemical reduction with NaBH4 [45,46]. Briefly, a mixture of 0.005 mol of AgNO3, 100 ml of Deionized (DI) water, and 0.05 g of polyvinyl pyrrolidone (as a stabilizer) was vigorously stirred for 60 min. Then, Ag NPs were prepared by gradually increasing 0.05 mol NaBH4 and stirring it for 30 min. These Ag NPs were washed with DI water, separated by centrifugation, and dried in a vacuum at 80 °C for 24 h.

For the preparation of Au NPs, hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O) was prepared from metallic gold (24-karat gold bar was bought from the gold shop of Mashhad in Iran) as detailed procedure given in S1. Then, Au NPs were prepared by reduction of HAuCl4·3H2O with NaBH4 and sodium citrate as a capping agent. For this purpose, 10 ml of 0.5 mM trisodium citrate dihydrate solution was added to 10 ml of 0.5 mM HAuCl4 solution and stirred for 10 min (solution A). Then, 0.6 ml of 0.1 M cold NaBH4 was added dropwise into solution A and stirred for 2 h. After that, 0.008 mol of cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) was added to 200 ml of 0.5 mM HAuCl4 and heated at 50 °C for 15 min under strong magnetic stirring (solution B). After the appearance of a clear solution, 0.1 ml of 0.1 M ascorbic acid solution was added to a mixture of 18 ml of solution B and 2 ml of solution A and stirred for 15 min. The Au NPs coated with CTAB were separated from the solution and twice rinsed with DI water and then dried in an oven naturally. The average particle size of prepared Au NPs is 14 nm as shown in TEM image in Fig. S5. The NMs of Ag@TiO2, Ag@TG, BaV, GQDs, RGO, and TG NPs are prepared similarly to our previous works [22,47,48] as given briefly in S1.

For the preparation of Ag doped VO2 (m) NPs (AgV), 0.005 mol vanadium (V) oxide was mixed with 0.01 mol oxalic acid dihydrate and 40 ml DI water. This solution was sonicated in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min after adding 1.25 mmol AgNO3 and 0.2 mmol CTAB. The obtained yellowish slurry was heated in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 180 °C for 48 h. The precipitate was centrifuged, washed with DI water and ethanol, and freeze-dried for 24 h. The obtained powder was annealed in a quartz tube furnace under vacuum (10−2 mTorr) for 6 h.

The FTO substrate was cleaned using a series of solvents in the following order: distilled water, ethanol, acetone, and isopropyl alcohol. Each cleaning step was performed for 15 min in an ultrasound bath. The FTO substrate was then dried at 100 °C for 30 min and heated at 450 °C for 30 min to effectively remove any organic contaminants. After cleaning, the FTO was used as substrate for fabricating the photoelectrode. Fig. 1 illustrates the fabrication route of photoanodes. The cleaned FTO was coated with the compact TiO2 (c-TiO2) as a blocking layer in the acidic solution (0.2 M HCl) of 0.2 M Titanium (IV) isopropoxide (TTIP) using a spin coater at 3000 rpm for 20 s. Then, it was annealed at 500 °C for 30 min (Fig. 1A).

The RGO, TG, Ag@TiO2, Ag@TG, AgV, and BaV layers were coated by electrophoretic deposition (EPD) that Pt plate (1.6 cm2) and FTO/c-TiO2 in the distance of 2 cm from each other. The EPD of the RGO layer on the FTO/c-TiO2 was carried out at 20 V for 2 min using 2 mg RGO in 10 ml DI water (Fig. 1B). The layer was putted in a vacuum oven at 70 °C. The EPD of the layers containing TiO2 was performed by 12 mg from each of these NMs with 3 mg I2 dispersed in acetone at 40 V for 2 min (Fig. 1C). The layers containing VO2 were fabricated by suspension 5 mg of their powder and 5 mg I2 in 15 ml acetone at 40 V for 4 min (Fig. 1D).

Ag NPs were coated using PVD and spin coater. PVD deposition was carried out using silver granule 99.0 % with the vapor flux 2–3 Å s−1 to achieve film thicknesses of nearly 15 and 25 nm. Ag, Au NPs, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were spin coated from an aqueous solution containing 0.001 mol of NPs at 500 rpm for 10 s.

The prepared photoanodes are named according to the name of the layer(s) putted above the c-TiO2 layer, each layer is specified from its upper layer by a slash (/). For example, see the schematic of illustrated photoanode in Fig. 1 labeled as RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG.

The XRD patterns of TiO2, Ag@TiO2, TG, Ag@TG, BaV, and AgV are indicated in Fig. 2 a. The XRD pattern of Ag@TiO2 and TG is similar to that of the TiO2 anatase phase with a tetragonal crystal structure (JCPDS Card no. 04-0477, the diffraction peaks related to TiO2 can be seen in Fig. S6). The XRD pattern of TG or Ag@TG did not show a diffraction peak of GQDs due to the small amount present in the nanocomposite and their low crystallinity. The Ag-doped TiO2 NPs clearly exhibit the diffraction peaks of the metallic Ag and show a face-centered cubic structure of metallic Ag at 2θ = 44.5 [49,50] marked as Ag in Fig. 2a. The XRD pattern of Ag@TiO2 and Ag@TG showed additional peaks at 2θ = 27.87°, 32.23°, 46.18°, and 57.52°, which are attributed to the rutile phase of TiO2 (JCPDS:065–1119).

The XRD pattern of BaV is associated with to M-phase of VO2, (JCPDS, no. 43-1051). There is no obvious any diffraction peak for Ba NPs (Fig. 2a). However, the XPS analysis for BaV confirms that Ba2+ ions were successfully doped in VO2 (M) lattice, see Fig. 2b. In the high-resolution XPS spectra, the binding energies of 779.3 and 794.7 eV with the spin–orbit splitting energy difference of 15.4 eV represent the reported literature value of the core level of Ba 3d which splits into two components of 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively.

The XRD pattern of AgV showed the diffraction peaks related to two M (JCPDS Card JCPDS, No. 43-1051) and B (JCPDS Card No. 81–2392) phases of VO2. Moreover, Ag were well doped in VO2 structure because the additional diffraction peaks positioned at 38.1, 43.8, 65.3, and 77.7° belong to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystal planes of face-centered cubic Ag, respectively [35]. The XRD patterns of GQDs and RGO are shown in Fig. S7.

To specify the alignment of the conduction and valence bands of the samples relative to each other, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the RGO, Ag@TG, BaV, and AgV were recorded (Fig. 2c) and their band gaps were determined using Tauc plot (Fig. 2d). The calculated band gaps, obtained from Tauc plot intercept, for AgV, BaV, RGO, Ag@TiO2, and Ag@TG are 2.5, 2.2, 2.8, 2.7, and 2.4 eV, respectively.

TEM images of GQDs, TiO2,TG, Ag@TG, Ag, RGO and BaV NPs are shown in Fig. 3 . The TEM image of GQDs (Fig. 3a) reveals the separated dots with an average particle size of 20 nm. Fig. 3b shows a representative distribution of TiO2 NPs, while Fig. 3c confirms the successful embedding of TiO2 on GQDs. Although it is not feasible to specifically confirm the presence of Ag in the TEM images of Ag@TG due to its low amount compared to TiO2, the existence of Ag in the structure of Ag@TG is proven by the FE-SEM image in Fig. 4 . The TEM image of Ag NPs shows monodispersed spheres with an almost distinct boundary for adjacent NPs, and an average particle size of Ag NPs of 25 nm. The TEM image of BaV (Fig. 3g) shows grainy smooth particles, which is similar to what can be observed in its FE-SEM image (Fig. 3h). Although the existence of Ba is not clearly identified in the TEM images of BaV, it is confirmed by EDX analyses in Fig. 3i and XPS analyses in Fig. 2b.

The FE-SEM images for top-view of Ag@TG, BaV, and AgV photoanodes with low and high magnifications and their elemental maps at high magnifications are displayed in Fig. 4. The Ag@TG photoanode shows that the NPs are aggregated with a grainy shape (Fig. 4a and b). Also, the surface of BaV (Fig. 4c) shows NPs have agglomerated with a round shape. Moreover, in the low-magnification FE-SEM image of Ag@TG can be seen grainy smooth particles with larger sizes which are attributed to BaV (as seen in Fig. 4b). The presence of V and Ba elements in surface of Ag@TG were confirmed by related elemental mappings in Fig. 4. Hence, the Ag@TG layer has infiltrated into the BaV layer resulting in joined contact and supreme adhesion between the layers as well as the participation of the BaV layer in visible light absorption.

The AgV NPs form a uniform layer with successive holes during the EPD as shown in Fig. 4e. This is similar to the EPD of doped VO2 (m) with earth alkali metals layer in our previous work [22]. According to FE-SEM image with high-magnification (Fig. 4f), the belt-like AgV NPs are irregular and aggregated with widths less than 100 nm and different lengths.

To analyze the quality of contact Ag located at the interface of layers, the top view of the FE-SEM image of BaV/Ag assembly is represented in Fig. 5 a, which can be proved by the elemental mapping. Obviously, Ag NPs with smaller sizes (orang points in Fig. 5a based on the elemental mapping of Ag NPs, especially in the marked area with a dotted line) fully distributed at the surface of BaV, giving an evidence for the successful fabrication of intimate interfacial contact between the Ag NPs and the BaV layer. Therefore, an electric contact between Ag@TG and BaV layers leads to fabricate an efficient and stable Z-scheme system. Besides, the FE-SEM image from cross-section of BaV/Ag/Ag@TG shows a joined contact and supreme adhesion between the layers (Fig. 5b). The boundary of layers is not clear. The junction of layers shows a dense strip-like layer.

Fig. 6 a shows the LSV curves (Jph vs. E) for fabricated photoanodes. The Jph for all photoanodes in dark is almost equal to zero due to the presence of a thin layer of c-TiO2. The c-TiO2 layer has the potential to serve as an effective hole-blacking layer, given that it possesses suitable band alignment and good transparency. Additionally, its good adhesion to FTO and high density can enhance the electron transfer pathways and create a hole-mirroring effect. These factors collectively contribute to the improved performance of the photoelectrode [51,52].

In our previous work [23], the Jph of TiO2 photoanode was improved by the synchronic presence of GQDs and doped Ag in TiO2. Also, an additional increment in Jph (1.500 mA cm−2) was attained using coating Ag@TG with VO2 doped with Ba due to the type II band alignment between BaV and Ag@TG. From Fig. 6a, the following improvements of the present work can be concluded:
(1)
All photoanodes show photoelectrocatalytic activity and the ordering of Jph for photoanodes at 1.23 VRHE are as follows:

RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG > BaV/Ag/Ag@TG ≥ BaV/Au/Ag@TG > Ag@TG/BaV > BaV/Ag/TG > BaV/RGO/Ag@TG > Ag@TG/AgV > Ag@TG > TG/AgV > BaV/GQDs/Ag@TG > AgV/Ag/TG > TiO2.
(2)
The BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode displays a higher Jph and a lower onset potential (Eonset ) compared to Ag@TG/BaV indicating that Z-scheme system between Ag@TG and BaV has more ability in charge transfer and separation process than that of their type II heterojunction. In addition, the redox ability in the Z-scheme system is strongly preserved due to the maintenance of the photoinduced holes on more positive potential (VB of Ag@TG) and electrons on more negative (CB of BaV). Moreover, the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Ag NPs could broaden the response range of the photoanode in visible region. In fact, Ag NPs acted as both an electron mediator and a photosensitizer.
(3)
The BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode exhibits a significant increase in Jph , 1.23 V compared to the BaV/Ag@TG. It is because the Ag NPs located at the interface of the BaV and Ag@TG as charge transport channels facilitate the charge transfer and separation process. In other words, the combined utilization of Ag NPs within the TG structure and at the interface of BaV and Ag@TG leads to enhanced separation of photogenerated charge carriers and prolonged carrier lifetime, as evidenced by the obtained results. The presence of Ag NPs within the TG structure contributes to improved light absorption, primarily due to the SPR effect observed in the UV–Vis spectrum, as well as efficient charge carrier separation within the TiO2 bulk. Additionally, the inclusion of Ag NPs between the two layers establishes a close connection between them, facilitating effective electron channeling and efficient charge transfer at the interface. Furthermore, Ag NPs at the interface facilitate and strengthen the SPR effect of Ag NPs in TG structure resulting in effectively accelerating the separation of photogenerated carrier.
(4)
The TG/AgV and AgV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanodes display a lower Jph , 1.23 V over Ag@TG/BaV. This decrease may be because the band alignment between TG and AgV could not offer efficient spatial separation of the e−/h+ pairs.
(5)
More importantly, The BaV/RGO/Ag@TG or BaV/GQDs/Ag@TG indicated a lower Jph, 1.23 V over BaV/Ag/Ag@TG. Therefore, carbon-based materials as electron mediator are not very (so) desirable for the construction of Z-scheme systems between Ag@TG and BaV. It is probably due to a poor contact between RGO or GQDs with BaV and Ag@TG, as well as unfavorable band alignment between RGO or GQDs and them. Therefore, they can act as recombination centers instead of electron transfer bridges which leads to a noticeable reduction in photocatalytic/photoelectrocatalytic activity.
(6)
The RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode showed the highest Jph , 1.23 V and the most negative Eonset among photoanodes. This enhancement can be attributed to the formation of the Z-scheme system as well as the fast transfer of photogenerated electrons to the FTO surface by RGO layer. This is because RGO has stronger charge mobility and electron acceptance. In other words, the RGO layer could capture and rapidly transport the photogenerated electrons from the CB of BaV to FTO surface, thanks to its structure that can act as electron reservoir, and its suitable band alignment in this photoanode structure, as observed in energy band diagram of the photoanode. In addition, the NPs were successfully dispersed without agglomeration on the RGO layer, resulting in better performance and effective contact between of the photocatalysts NPs.

It is worth mentioning that the Jph dropped substantially when Ag NPs layer was deposited by the PVD method (Fig. S8). It may be due to the lack of an appropriate contact between BaV and Ag@TG layers, light screening effect as well as encouraging the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes rather than transfer them. Therefore, the efficiency of photoanodes strongly depends on the deposition method of layers and the amount Ag loading.

The computed ISTC efficiencies are plotted against Jphoto (Fig. 6b). As shown in this figure, the RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode shows the maximum ISTC efficiency. The high ISTC efficiency of this photoande may be attributed to the synergistic effects of formation of the indirect Z-scheme system between Ag@TG and BaV, the presence of dopped Ag in TG, and compositing TiO2 with GQDs. In addition, the increased efficiency of RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG over BaV/Ag/Ag@TG confirms the prominent performance of RGO in charge transfer to FTO. In fact, by engineering in this photoanode not only the capacity for visible light absorption increases but also the separation and transfer of the photogenerated charge carriers with maintaining their strong redox ability promote. Hence, the photogenerated charge carriers can be used efficiently and facilitated the water oxidation. The order of increase in ISTC efficiency of photoanodes is related to the increasing trend of Jph of photoanodes. The conversion efficiency of photoanodes when RGO or GQDs are utilized as the electron mediator is significantly lower by 4.5 or 3.0 times than that of the Ag NPs mediator, respectively.

The transient photocurrent density was determined at 1.23 V vs. RHE under repeated on/off illuminations (Fig. 6c). All photoanodes showed fast and reproducible photoresponse under visible light so that Jph abruptly raised in on illuminations. In off situation, it dropped sharply to zero or nearly zero. The RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG exhibited the highest photocurrent intensity, implying the highest photoinduced electron generation efficiency, highly efficient separation, and more efficient migration of the photogenerated carriers among photoanodes. Moreover, the photocurrent density of BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode is larger than that of Ag@TG/BaV. This results confirms that Z-scheme system between Ag@TG and BaV have better charge separation efficiency than its type-II heterojunction system.

The photoelectrocatalytic durability of photoanodes was assessed by measurement Jph in 3 h. As shown in Fig. 6d, the stability of photoelectrodes is admitted during the experiments. The photoanodes with the overlying TiO2 such as RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG indicate supreme stability during the experiment. These photoanodes are stable for more than 6 h due to firm adhesion of their layers. The stability of the photoanodes with the overlying VO2 such as Ag@TG/BaV is acceptable for approximately 2 h but after that, an apparent decline is observed due to the etching effect from the surface of these photoanodes. Therefore, the RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode has not only high efficiency but also it has excellent stability making it a suitable option for practical applications.

Fig. 7 presents the chopped LSV, ηsep, ηinj and PL to further analyze the differences in PEC performance among the various photoanodes. Fig. 7a shows the photocurrent response under chopped light for the photoanodes. The transient photocurrent for RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode is negligible, indicating that the holes can more efficiently transfer at the photoelectrode/electrolyte interface. The transient photocurrent for other photoanodes can be observed at potentials less than 1.5 V.

As shown in Fig. 7b, the recombination process is remarkably suppressed in the RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode. The significant improvement in ηsep observed in the RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG and BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanodes is mainly attributed to the improved interconnection between the layers due to the presence of Ag.

The charge injection efficiency was determined to provide useful information on the charge transport on the surface. As shown in Fig. 7c, the ηinj value for the RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode is higher than that of the BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode. This suggests that RGO acts as an efficient modifier that facilitates the transportation of photogenerated electrons to the counter electrode through the external circuit, while allowing more holes with high redox ability to participate in the PEC water oxidation at the photoanode/electrolyte interface. This leads to a significantly improved photocurrent in the RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode.

Comparing BaV/Ag/Ag@TG with Ag@TG/BaV, the former exhibits higher ηsep and ηinj (as shown in Fig.7b and 7c), which are about 40 % and 60 % greater than those of the Ag@TG/BaV electrode at 1.23 V vs RHE, respectively. These results fully support the hypothesis that the indirect Z-scheme system facilitates carrier migration. In other words, inserting Ag NPs between BaV and Ag@TG can form a contact with low contact resistance, which enables effective carrier interface transfer and spatial separation under light excitation conditions. This point is further supported by the PL emission spectra shown in Fig. 7d. The low-intensity PL spectrum indicates efficient separation of the charge carriers, resulting in the participation of more electrons and holes in the oxidation and reduction reactions. This is because the PL emission spectrum originates from the recombination of free carriers. The intensity of the PL spectrum for the RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanodes is significantly lower than that of other photoanodes, indicating that the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs can migrate easily between BaV and Ag@TG, due to the matching band potentials and the presence of Ag NPs.

The movement path of the photogenerated charge carrier was elucidated by the OCP transient tests of photoanodes (Fig. 7e). The negative increase in voltage under light irradiation was observed for all photoanodes [53–55]. Hence, they have n-type characters so that the photogenerated electrons are migrated to the counter electrode. As observed in Fig. 7e, the most considerable photoelectric conversion capability and the largest photovoltage belong to the RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode due to its electron accumulation stability. In addition, the produced photovoltage of BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode is larger than that of Ag@TG/BaV which confirms its higher photoelectric conversion capability for visible light.

The positive slope of Mott-Schottky plots for AgV, BaV, Ag@TiO2, and Ag@TG photoelectrodes (Fig. 7f) suggests that all are n-type semiconductors and have Efb close to the CB. The Efb of these photoelectrodes is determined through extrapolating the linear part of Mott-Schottky plot to y = 0 and it can be observed as the CB of the energy band diagram in Fig. 8 a.

The energy band diagram and charge transfer process of RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode according to their measured bandgaps and CB edges are suggested in Fig. 8a. As this figure displays, the electron flow direction elucidated based on Z-scheme system existing between BaV and Ag@TG with the assistance of Ag NPs at the interface of them as solid-state electron mediator for the transfer of electrons from the conduction band of Ag@TG to the valence band BaV. Upon irradiation, the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs appeared in both layers of BaV and Ag@TG. The photogenerated electrons in the CB of Ag@TG were easily shifted into Ag NPs and recombined with the photoinduced holes in the VB of BaV. In addition, the photoelectrons in CB of BaV were rapidly transferred to the FTO surface by inserted RGO layer between BaV layer and surface. Then, the photoinduced electrons through the external circuit are transferred to the counter electrode to produce hydrogen. Also, the photogenerated holes remained in VB of Ag@TG for participation in the oxygen evolution reaction. These lead to highly efficient e−/h+ separation and enhancement in the photoelectrocatalytic activity. In other words, the accumulation of electrons and holes at the highest possible CB and lowest VB in this system provides strong reduction and oxidation abilities.

To further investigate the interfacial charge transfer abilities and the effect of Ag NPs sites on the charge transfer kinetics behavior of photoanodes, the EIS of AgV/Ag/TG, Ag@TG/BaV, BaV/Ag/Ag@TG, and RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanodes are compared in Fig. 8b. As the result of our previous work [22], the Ag@TG with the smallest arc diameter has the excellent charge transfer and separation rate in comparison with those of Ag@TiO2 and TG. As depicted in Fig. 8b, the smallest impedance arc radius belongs to the RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG. It is due to the lower electron transfer resistance which accelerated interfacial charge transfer and allowed efficient separation of e−/h+ pairs in this photoanodes. Also, the impedance arc radius of BaV/Ag/Ag@TG photoanode is smaller than that of Ag@TG/BaV that confirms a faster interfacial charge transfer rate in the Z-scheme system. Thus, the EIS results are in accordance with other photoelectrochemical measurements, implying that forming Z-scheme system between BaV and Ag@TG improved the capacity of photo-induced charge generation and transfer, redox ability, and separation of e−/h+ pairs. This is due to the easy channelization of electrons and acceleration of the separation of e−/h+ pairs through an intimate contact between BaV and Ag@TG and the presence of Ag NPs in the Z-scheme system resulted in a highly enhanced photoelectrocatalytic performance. Also, the arc radius is greater when Ag NPs presented in VO2 layer and layers interface. It originates from the lack of suitable band alignment between AgV and TG or Ag@TG.

In this work, the Z-scheme system was formed by altering the position of Ag NPs and coupling TiO2 with BaV. The highest photoelectrochemical performance was achieved with the BaV/Ag/Ag@TG assembly, where Ag NPs were at the interface. However, no significant improvement was observed when a layer of VO2 doped with Ag NPs. In addition, various electron transfer mediators including Au NPs, Ag NPs, RGO, and GQDs were examined using different deposition methods. The efficiency of photoanodes is strongly dependent on the deposition method, likely due to the amount and quality of contact area between BaV and Ag@TG. Interestingly, while Ag NPs facilitate charge transfer between the layers, the overlying Ag@TG layer effectively stabilizes the BaV layer due to its stronger adhesion of the TiO2 layer. Finally, the photoanode with sequentially assembled RGO/BaV/Ag/Ag@TG on the FTO surface revealed a high Jph of 2.15 mA cm−2 (30 times higher than that of pristine TiO2 photoanode (0.07 mA cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE)) which is 10 times higher and more stable than the type-II heterojunction with RGO/Ag@TG/BaV assembly (1.5 mA cm−2) to pristine TiO2. This is because the Z-scheme system formation between BaV and Ag@TG is more effective in separating e−/h+ pairs and maintaining redox capability.

The findings of this study highlight the importance of utilizing an electron transfer mediator as a promising strategy for forming a Z-scheme formation between semiconductors with the ability to construct a type-II heterojunction, which can enhance the PEC performance of photoelectrocatalysts. Also, there is potential for improving photocurrents by optimizing the interface between the layers using other deposition methods of electron transfer mediators in future researchers. This will help to further enhance the efficiency of PEC water oxidation reactions, ultimately leading to the advancement of functional and highly efficient photoelectrocatalysts.

Halimeh-Sadat Sajjadizadeh: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Elaheh K. Goharshadi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation. Mahdi Karimi-Nazarabad: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation.
